document_text:
Quantifying Membership Disclosure Risk
for Tabular Synthetic Data Using Kernel Density Estimators
Rajdeep Pathak
Department of Mathematics
Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad
Telangana, India 502285
Email: pathak.rajdeep@alumni.iith.ac.inSayantee Jana
Department of Mathematics
Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad
Telangana, India 502285
Email: sayantee.jana@math.iith.ac.in
Abstract—The use of synthetic data has become increasingly
popular as a privacy-preserving alternative to sharing real
datasets, especially in sensitive domains such as healthcare,
finance, and demography. However, the privacy assurances
of synthetic data are not absolute, and remain susceptible
to membership inference attacks (MIAs), where adversaries
aim to determine whether a specific individual was present
in the dataset used to train the generator. In this work, we
propose a practical and effective method to quantify mem-
bership disclosure risk in tabular synthetic datasets using
kernel density estimators (KDEs). Our KDE-based approach
models the distribution of nearest-neighbour distances between
synthetic data and the training records, allowing probabilistic
inference of membership and enabling robust evaluation via
ROC curves. We propose two attack models: a ‘True Distribu-
tion Attack’, which assumes privileged access to training data,
and a more realistic, implementable ‘Realistic Attack’ that
uses auxiliary data without true membership labels. Empirical
evaluations across four real-world datasets and six synthetic
data generators demonstrate that our method consistently
achieves higher F1 scores and sharper risk characterization
than a prior baseline approach, without requiring compu-
tationally expensive shadow models. The proposed method
provides a practical framework and metric for quantifying
membership disclosure risk in synthetic data, which enables
data custodians to conduct a post-generation risk assessment
prior to releasing their synthetic datasets for downstream
use. The datasets and codes for this study are available at
https://github.com/PyCoder913/MIA-KDE.
1. Introduction
Imagine sharing rich and sensitive healthcare, financial,
or demographic data without risking individual privacy -
synthetic data generation makes this possible. By training
statistical or Machine Learning models on real data to
simulate datasets, synthetic data aims to retain analytical
utility while protecting individual identities. However, de-
spite growing adoption, synthetic datasets remain suscep-
tible to membership inference attacks (MIAs) [1], where
adversaries infer whether specific individuals were presentin the original training data. This is particularly concerning
when the inclusion of an individual in the dataset reveals
sensitive information (e.g., HIV status, rare diseases, finan-
cial default).
State-of-the-art MIAs typically employ computationally
expensive shadow modelling strategies [2], [3], [4], training
multiple shadow generators to simulate attack scenarios and
meta-classifiers for membership prediction. A more practical
alternative uses distance-based methods that calculate F1
scores via data partitioning [5], [6], [7], but these produce
hard membership labels rather than probabilistic predictions.
In this work, we introduce a non-parametric, distance-
based framework using Kernel Density Estimators (KDEs)
to model empirical distance distributions between synthetic
and training data. Extending the methodology used in [5],
[6], [7], our approach generates probabilistic membership
predictions, enabling improved analysis through ROC curves
at low false positive rates while remaining computationally
efficient. Our contributions are threefold: (1) a KDE-based
privacy scoring framework for tabular synthetic data, (2)
comprehensive validation across diverse datasets and gener-
ation models, and (3) demonstration of superior F1 scores
compared to the existing data-partitioning method without
requiring expensive shadow model training.
2. Background and Related Work
2.1. Synthetic Data Generation
Synthetic data is generated by first training a statistical
or machine learning model on the original dataset, and
then producing artificial (synthetic) records by sampling
new values from the learned model. Contemporary liter-
ature highlights diverse methodologies for synthetic data
generation. Bayesian networks (e.g., BayNet, PrivBayes [8])
model attribute dependencies via directed acyclic graphs
and conditionals. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-
based approaches like CTGAN (Conditional Tabular GAN)
[9] use adversarial training to capture complex data distribu-
tions, though they face challenges in tabular data synthesis.
Generative models such as Variational Autoencoders (V AEs)arXiv:2603.10937v1  [cs.LG]  11 Mar 2026

[10], [11] and diffusion models [12] have gained signifi-
cant attention in synthetic data generation. Recently, Large
Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising
results in synthesizing tabular data, capturing patterns and
distributions inherent in real-world datasets.
2.2. Membership Inference Attacks
Membership inference attacks (MIAs) have emerged as a
standard approach for assessing the privacy risks associated
with synthetic data and Machine Learning models. For
synthetic tabular data, one class of MIAs involves direct
comparisons between synthetic and original records to iden-
tify exact or near matches [13]. However, these similarity-
based approaches have been criticized for underestimating
privacy risks [3]. The state-of-the-art alternatives are shadow
modelling approaches [2], [3], [4], which are practically
infeasible for large datasets due to their heavy computa-
tional overhead. For instance, consider a data custodian who
routinely generates and sells synthetic datasets derived from
proprietary, high-dimensional, and dynamic data containing
billions of records, refreshed weekly. To assess privacy risk
before each release, employing shadow modelling would
require training numerous additional generative models - an
approach that is both time and resource-intensive. Given the
dynamic nature of the data, any delay in release diminishes
its utility and commercial value to potential users.
Breugel et al. present DOMIAS [14], a membership
inference attack against generative models, which estimates
synthetic data and reference data distributions using methods
like BNAF and KDE. It can be used as a loss during the
training of the generative model, as well as a post-training
metric to evaluate the synthetic data MI risk. A more popular
approach implemented in practice to evaluate membership
disclosure risks of synthetic data is a distance-based ap-
proach [1], [5], [6], [7], which we elaborate in Section 3.1.
Our proposed method is grounded on the distance-based
method, where we model the distance distributions using
KDEs.
2.3. Kernel Density Estimation
Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), also known as the
Parzen’s window [15], is a fundamental nonparametric tech-
nique used to estimate the probability density function (pdf)
of a random variable based on a finite data sample. Un-
like parametric models, KDE does not assume a specific
functional form for the underlying distribution, making it
especially useful for exploring complex, multimodal, or
unknown distributions. Given a sampleX 1, . . . , X n∈Rd
drawn independently from an unknown distribution with
densityp(x), the kernel density estimator at a pointx∈Rd
is defined as,
ˆpn(x) =1
nhdnX
i=1Kx−X i
h
,
whereK:Rd→Ris a smooth, symmetric kernel func-
tion (e.g., Gaussian or Epanechnikov), andh >0is thebandwidth (smoothing parameter) controlling the scale of
smoothing.
3. Methodology
3.1. Motivation
This section discusses the practical approach for quan-
tifying membership disclosure risk from El Emam et al. [5]
and establishes our proposed framework.
Membership inference attacks simulate a classification
task where adversaries classify records as training set mem-
bers or non-members. Given real datasetR(training set)
generating synthetic dataS, and unseen dataU(not used
in training), we form an attack datasetD attack comprising
records fromRandU. The standard approach in [1], [5],
[6], [7] (which we hereafter refer to asMethod 1) sets a
distance thresholdτ, computes nearest-neighbour distances
betweenD attack records and synthetic dataS, and classifies
records below the threshold as members, and others as
non-members. Finally, the F1 score for this classification
quantifies the membership disclosure risk.
However, Carlini et al. [16] argue that average-case
metrics like F1 scores underestimate privacy risks by mask-
ing worst-case leakage scenarios. They recommend ROC
analysis evaluating true positive rates (TPRs) at low false
positive rates (FPRs). Method 1 produces only hard clas-
sifications, not probabilistic membership scores needed for
comprehensive ROC analysis. In many settings, it might be
beneficial to have a probability associated with the class
label. Membership probabilities provide a sense of confi-
dence, the degree of uncertainty, or the likelihood of being
incorrect in classifying records as members.
We propose a KDE-based approach that retains Method
1’s structure and generates probabilistic membership predic-
tions. Our method models the relationship between nearest-
neighbour distances and membership probability using real
training data, synthetic data, and unseen holdout data. We
present two variants: theTrue Distribution Attack(lever-
aging data holders’ privileged access to training data for
risk assessment) and theRealistic Attack(using auxiliary
datasets accessible to adversaries, which is also a require-
ment in some prior works [2], [5]). The interpretation of
membership inference risk is two-fold: low risk may indicate
genuine privacy protection, or reflect weak attack models
that underestimate actual leakage. While our approach may
not match shadow model accuracies [4], it offers significant
practical advantages in terms of computation time.
3.2. True Distribution Attack
LetRdenote the real training dataset,Sthe synthetic
dataset, andUunseen records not used during training. We
construct the attack datasetD attack by combiningRandU,
with ground truth labels:Rrecords as members (class = 1)
andUrecords as non-members (class = 0). We randomly
splitD attack into balanced training and test sets - each set

has an equal number of members(∈R)and non-members
(∈U), ensuring 50% baseline accuracy and 0.67 baseline
F1 score (random guess1).
We compute Gower’s distance2[17] between each record
inD attack and its nearest neighbour inS, creating distance ta-
bleD distswith computed distances and corresponding mem-
bership labels. We fit separate KDEs to member distances
(KDE member ) and non-member distances (KDE non-member ),
providing smooth approximations of empirical distance dis-
tributions for each class.
For evaluation, we classify test records by computing
their nearest-neighbour distancedto the synthetic dataset
and estimating membership probability using:
P(member|d) =KDE member (d)
KDE member (d) +KDE non-member (d)(1)
This estimator, formally justified by Bayes’ Theorem
(Proposition 3.1), models membership likelihood without
fixed distance thresholds. This probability can be thresh-
olded (e.g.,P(member|d)≥0.5) to classify whether the
test record is a member. The probabilistic output enables
both classification metrics (accuracy, F1) and comprehensive
ROC analysis. Figure 1 illustrates this process.
Figure 1. A visual depiction of the process flow of the True Distribution
Attack: First, the attack dataset is constructed using training and unseen
records; For each record in the attack dataset, its nearest neighbour distance
from the synthetic data is obtained; Separate KDEs are fitted to the member
distances and non-member distances.
Proposition 3.1.Following the above setup, assume that
KDE member andKDE non-member approximates the distribu-
tions of member distances and non-member distances re-
spectively. Then for a given query record and the distanced
of its nearest neighbour in the synthetic data, the probability
that it is a member of the training set is given by
P(member|d) =KDE member (d)
KDE member (d) +KDE non-member (d)
1. In a random guess attack, an adversary can achieve a baseline accuracy
of 50% by randomly guessing whether a record is a member or non-
member, from a set of records having an equal number of members and
non-members.
2. Absolute difference (Manhattan distance) for numerical features; 0 for
matching categories, 1 otherwise.Proof.Assume that the prior probabilities of the query
record being a member and non-member are equal, i.e.,
P(member) =P(non-member) = 0.5. From Bayes’ Theo-
rem, we have
P(member|d) =P(d|member)×P(member)
P(d)(2)
By the Theorem of Total Probability, it follows that the
probability of observing the distancedis given by:
P(d) =P(d|member)P(member)
+P(d|non-member)P(non-member)
=P(member) [P(d|member) +P(d|non-member)]
Putting this in the denominator of equation 2, we get that
P(member|d) =
=P(d|member)×P(member)
P(member) [P(d|member) +P(d|non-member)]
=P(d|member)
P(d|member) +P(d|non-member)
≈KDE member (d)
KDE member (d) +KDE non-member (d)
Since,KDE member (d)andKDE non-member (d)approximate
the probabilitiesP(d|member)andP(d|non-member)re-
spectively.
When member and non-member distance distributions
are statistically indistinguishable, the true distribution attack
yields poor performance below baseline levels. For example,
TV AE-generated UK Census data shows statistically indis-
tinguishable distances (KS testp-value = 0.71), resulting in
accuracy (49.97%) and F1 score (0.375) below baselines.
While this suggests low average MIA risk which might be
a good news for the data custodian, ROC analysis (Figure
12) reveals high TPRs at low FPRs, indicating successful
attacks in worst-case scenarios (discussed in Section 5.2).
3.3. Realistic Attack
In practical scenarios, adversaries will not have access
to the true membership labels, making direct modelling of
member and non-member distance distributions impossible.
We propose theRealistic Attack, where adversaries access
only auxiliary datasets from the same population, potentially
containing mixed training and non-training records acquired
through public sources. The requirement of such an auxiliary
dataset does not undermine the validity of our approach,
because our objective is providing data custodians practical
risk assessment methods, and not creating new, potentially
stronger, MIAs per se.
We simulate this usingD attack (mixture ofRandU)
as auxiliary data. Without true labels, adversaries partition
records into‘supposed members’(with distance below
threshold) and‘supposed non-members’(above threshold),
based solely on nearest-neighbour distances to synthetic
data. Unlike Method 1, these labels are not used directly for
classification. Instead, we fit separate KDEs to the supposed

member and supposed non-member distances, and evaluate
using Equation 1, enabling probabilistic assessment under
realistic assumptions (Figure 2).
This yields monotonically decreasing membership prob-
abilities as functions of distances. Label noise occurs as sup-
posed member distances may include true non-members and
vice versa, introducing modelling inaccuracies. However,
our experiments show realistic attack risk can exceed true
distribution attack risk for certain thresholds and datasets
(Figure 3, Section 5.1). At higher thresholds (above 50th
percentile), our realistic attack significantly outperforms
Method 1 in F1 scores.
Figure 2. A visual depiction of the process flow of the Realistic Attack:
The attack dataset is constructed first. As the data holder, we know the true
membership labels, but the adversary does not; For each record in the attack
dataset, its nearest neighbour distancedfrom the synthetic data is obtained.
A distance thresholdτis set. A record is labelled as ‘supposed member’
if the distanced < τ, otherwise ‘supposed non-member’; Separate KDEs
are fitted to the ‘supposed member distances’ and ‘supposed non-member
distances.’
4. Results
We use four publicly available datasets to demonstrate
our method: The MIMIC-IV data [18], [19], UK Census
data [20], Texas-100X data [21], and Nexoid COVID-19
data [22]. For all datasets, we partition the data into two
equal parts to construct the attack dataset: one half (R) is
used to train the generative models and generateS, while
the remaining half (U), unseen during training, is used as
the unseen (non-members) set. We use six different gen-
eration techniques: CTGAN [9], ADS-GAN [23], DPGAN
[24], TabDDPM [12], TV AE [9], and Bayesian Network
[25], which are trained onRand implemented using the
SynthCity framework [26].
We evaluate both attack variants across these 24 datasets
using Gower’s distance betweenD attack andS, measuring
MIA risk via attack accuracy, F1 score, and log-scaled ROC
curves. First, we formD distsby computing the distances
between records inD attack andS. We randomly splitD dists
into 70% training (KDE fitting) and 30% testing (balanced
members and non-members), using0.5membership proba-
bility threshold for classification and Scott’s rule for KDE
bandwidths. The specific choice of bandwidth matters in
practice, but the analysis and results hold for any choice. Fortrue distribution attack, we use equal numbers of members
and non-members in the training subset. Table 1 shows
training and test set sizes across datasets.
TABLE 1. NUMBER OF TRAINING AND TEST DISTANCES FOR EACH
DATASET.
MIMIC-IV UK Census Texas-100X Nexoid
# Training distances 112,000 398,818 647,588 420,000
# Test distances 48,000 170,923 277,540 180,000
Tables 2 and 3 report the accuracies and F1 scores, re-
spectively, for the true distribution attack across the various
datasets and generative models. In majority of the cases,
the synthetic datasets generated using Bayesian Network are
observed to be comparatively more vulnerable to member-
ship inference attacks, consistently exhibiting higher accu-
racy and F1 scores. Following Carlini et al. [16], we also
analyze ROC curves to assess worst-case privacy leakage
(see Section 5.2).
TABLE 2. MEMBERSHIP INFERENCE ACCURACIES FOR THE TRUE
DISTRIBUTION ATTACK ON VARIOUS DATASETS AND GENERATORS.
CTGAN ADS-GAN DPGAN TabDDPM TV AE Bayesian Network
MIMIC-IV 75.617% 73.708% 59.433% 79.508% 88.952% 68.298%
UK Census 50.075% 49.918% 57.933% 50.055% 49.975% 62.889%
Texas-100X 83.957% 71.184% 71.023% 50.49% 78.341% 97.503%
Nexoid 50.827% 50.353% 50.057% 51.6% 53.768% 58.493%
TABLE 3. F1SCORES FOR THE TRUE DISTRIBUTION ATTACK ON
VARIOUS DATASETS AND GENERATORS.
CTGAN ADS-GAN DPGAN TabDDPM TV AE Bayesian Network
MIMIC-IV 0.793 0.791 0.612 0.827 0.877 0.759
UK Census 0.333 0.402 0.274 0.633 0.375 0.631
Texas-100X 0.825 0.760 0.716 0.454 0.781 0.975
Nexoid 0.516 0.079 0.042 0.579 0.540 0.619
For the realistic attack, we conduct our experiments
using nine distinct distance thresholds, corresponding to the
10th,20th, . . . ,90thpercentiles of the nearest neighbour
distances. For each threshold, the distances are partitioned as
‘supposed member’ and ‘supposed non-member’ distances,
and separate KDEs are fitted to model the their distributions.
We report the resulting accuracy and F1 score achieved by
the realistic attack for each threshold, and compare these
with the corresponding scores obtained using Method 1
through heatmaps. We present the MIA risks (F1 scores)
for all three attacks (true distribution attack, realistic, and
method 1) across all four datasets and all generators in
Figure 3. Sections 4.1-4.3 present the results for the four
datasets with the help of a series of illustrative plots. The
fitted KDEs for the training distances of supposed members
and supposed non-members of the realistic attack for the
datasets are not included in the text for brevity. However,
they are available in theExperimentsnotebooks in the
GitHub repository for this project.
4.1. MIMIC-IV (EHR) Data
4.1.1. True Distribution Attack.Figure 4 presents ac-
curacy and F1 scores for synthetic MIMIC-IV datasets.

Figure 3. F1 scores (MIA risk) for different synthetic datasets across all
four real datasets: True Distribution Attack vs. Realistic vs. Method 1.
KS tests (p-values = 0) confirm statistically distinguish-
able member and non-member distance distributions for all
MIMIC-IV datasets. TV AE-generated data shows the highest
vulnerability with88.952%accuracy and0.877F1 score.
Figure 4. True distribution attack accuracies (left) and F1 scores (right) on
the MIMIC-IV synthetic datasets.
Figure 5 shows member/non-member distance distri-
butions, with fitted KDEs used to compute test record
membership probabilities. Figure 6 displays distance versus
membership probability relationships. Figure 7 presents log-
scaled ROC curves for true distribution attacks on EHR
synthetic datasets, demonstrating our method’s capability for
comprehensive ROC analysis through probabilistic predic-
tions.
Figure 5. MIMIC-IV data: Distribution of nearest-neighbour distances
between the training records (inD attack) and various synthetic datasets.
The member and non-member distances are modelled separately for each
synthetic data.
Figure 6. MIMIC-IV data: distance vs. membership probability for the test
records.
Figure 7. MIMIC-IV data: Log-ROC curves for the true distribution attack.
4.1.2. Realistic Attack.Figure 8 presents the realistic at-
tack results - the accuracies and F1 scores across nine
different distance thresholds. The realistic attack yields low
F1 scores at lower thresholds, indicating limited separability
between member and non-member records in these regions.
However, at higher thresholds, the F1 scores increase and
approach the baseline value of 0.67 (for more discussion,
see Section 5.1), and consistently outperform those achieved
by Method 1 under the same conditions. The accuracy and
F1 scores of both the realistic attack and Method 1 remain
below their respective baselines, suggesting that the realistic
attack is weaker in this case.
4.2. UK Census Data
The UK Census dataset contains only categorical fea-
tures, resulting in 7-14 discrete distance values and sparsity
in the membership probability plot (as fewer unique proba-
bilities are computed; see Figure 11). KS tests show indistin-
guishable member and non-member distances for CTGAN,
ADS-GAN, TabDDPM, and TV AE, with true distribution
attack accuracies near the 50% baseline, indicating reduced
MIA susceptibility from a data holder perspective.

Figure 8. MIMIC-IV data: Accuracies (top) and F1 scores (bottom) for
realistic attack vs. Method 1, across various distance thresholds.
Figure 9. True distribution attack accuracies (left) and F1 scores (right) on
the UK Census synthetic datasets.
Figure 10. UK Census data: Distribution of member and non-member
nearest-neighbour distances between the training records (inD attack) and
various synthetic datasets.
4.3. Texas-100X and Nexoid Data
For conciseness, we present only the ROC curves for the
true distribution attack on Texas-100X and Nexoid datasets,
as showing the corresponding KDE distributions and mem-
bership probability plots would be redundant. We also omit
the realistic attack heatmap for Nexoid data for brevity. They
are, however, available in our GitHub repository.
5. Discussion
Figure 11. UK Census data: distance vs. membership probability for the
test records.
Figure 12. UK Census data: Log-ROC curves for the true distribution
attack.
Figure 13. UK Census data: Accuracies (top) and F1 scores (bottom) for
realistic attack vs. Method 1, across various distance thresholds.
5.1. Distance Threshold vs. F1 Score for Realistic
Attack
Intuitively, realistic attack risk should be lower than true
distribution attack risk, as the latter uses true membership
labels to model distance distributions. However, we observe

Figure 14. Texas-100X data: Log-ROC curves for the true distribution
attack.
Figure 15. Texas-100X data: Accuracies (top) and F1 scores (bottom) for
realistic attack vs. Method 1, across various distance thresholds.
Figure 16. Nexoid data: Log-ROC curves for the true distribution attack.
that F1 scores from realistic attack can exceed F1 scores
of true distribution attack, at specific distance thresholds
(Figure 3). For UK Census data, this occurs across all syn-thetic datasets due to statistical indistinguishability between
member and non-member distance distributions, causing
true distribution attacks to perform near baseline levels. The
realistic attack achieves strong performance on Texas-100X
(F1 scores up to 0.98 for Bayesian Network at 50th-60th
percentiles) and shows generally increasing F1 scores with
higher thresholds for Nexoid data.
This trend reflects the relationship between TPs and FPs
as distance thresholds increase. Higher thresholds classify
more records as members, simultaneously increasing both
TPs and FPs. When TP growth outpaces FP growth, F1
scores improve, which is observed for UK Census, Texas-
100X, and Nexoid datasets (Figure 17). Texas-100X exhibits
the most pronounced TP-to-FP ratio increase, explaining
its high realistic attack F1 scores. MIMIC-IV presents an
exception: FPs initially grow faster than TPs, resulting in
lower F1 scores that approach the 0.67 baseline at higher
thresholds slowly, which is favorable for data custodians.
Attack effectiveness depends critically on dataset properties
and the privacy characteristics of specific synthetic data
generation methods.
Figure 17. Relationship between the number of true positives (X-axis) and
number of false positives (Y-axis) across the nine evaluated distance thresh-
olds. Each line corresponds to a different synthetic data generated using
the colour-coded generator. For each line, the leftmost point corresponds
to the TP-FP counts at the 10th percentile threshold, while the rightmost
point represents the same when the distance threshold is set to the 90th
percentile. The red dotted line represents the baseline(y=x), where the
TPs and FPs increase at the same rate. A point below the baseline indicates
that for that particular distance threshold percentile, the number of TPs is
more than that of FPs. Consequently, a line passing below the baseline
implies that TPs increase at a faster rate than the FPs as the threshold
increases, resulting in an improved F1 score. Note that, these plots are
different from ROC curves, where TPR is plotted on the Y-axis and FPR
on the X-axis.
5.2. ROC Analysis
Our approach enables seamless ROC analysis, revealing
critical limitations of average-case metrics like accuracy and
AUC in evaluating membership inference attacks [16]. For
example, TV AE-generated UK Census data achieves only

49.97% accuracy (below baseline), while DPGAN achieves
57.93% (8 percentage points above baseline), suggesting
limited risk. However, log-ROC analysis (Figure 12) re-
veals TPRs of 0.1-1.0 at FPR=10−6, indicating TPR up
to105times greater than FPR - a substantial vulnerabil-
ity masked by benign average metrics. Similarly, TV AE-
generated Nexoid data shows marginally above-baseline
performance (AUC=0.554, accuracy=53.77%), yet achieves
TPR=10−4-10−3at FPR=10−6(Figure 16), representing
TPR over 100 times higher than FPR. From a privacy
perspective, TPRs at low FPRs should not exceedt= 20
times the FPR (adjustable based on use-case requirements),
and metric selection should align with application-specific
risk tolerance and privacy requirements.
6. Conclusion
We proposed a computationally efficient KDE-based
framework for calculating membership disclosure risk in
tabular synthetic datasets, by modelling nearest-neighbour
distance distributions. Our method enables probabilistic
membership inference without expensive shadow modelling,
with further acceleration through distance calculations and
KDE estimation on GPUs. The proposed method primarily
functions as apost-generation metric, allowing data cus-
todians to assess risk after synthesis using only the training
data, synthetic dataset, and reference data from the same
population (a subset of the real data not used in training).
The experiments conducted in this study used balanced
attack datasets with equal member/non-member records.
However, El Emam et al. [5] suggest setting member pro-
portions ton/N(training size/population size) for realistic
deployment scenarios. Our implementation supports flexi-
ble partitioning and train-test-split parameters. Future work
could explore: (1) relaxing balanced dataset assumptions
to improve practical applicability, (2) investigating theoreti-
cal guarantees on distance-to-membership probability map-
pings, and (3) developing hybrid strategies combining KDE-
based post-hoc assessment with lightweight shadow mod-
elling or adversarial training for more robust membership
disclosure risk evaluation.
References

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of quantifying membership disclosure risk in tabular synthetic data using kernel density estimators. The main points covered include the introduction of synthetic data generation as a privacy-preserving alternative to sharing real datasets, the susceptibility of synthetic data to membership inference attacks, and the proposal of a practical and effective method to quantify membership disclosure risk using kernel density estimators. The document also highlights the limitations of existing methods, including computationally expensive shadow modeling strategies and distance-based methods that produce hard membership labels. The proposed approach generates probabilistic membership predictions, enabling improved analysis through ROC curves, and demonstrates superior F1 scores compared to existing methods. The document also provides an overview of synthetic data generation methodologies, including Bayesian networks, Generative Adversarial Networks, and Variational Autoencoders. Overall, the partial document presents a framework and metric for quantifying membership disclosure risk in synthetic data, enabling data custodians to conduct a post-generation risk assessment prior to releasing their synthetic datasets.

nodes.title: Synthetic Data Generation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of quantifying membership disclosure risk in tabular synthetic data using kernel density estimators. The main points covered include the introduction of synthetic data generation as a privacy-preserving alternative to sharing real datasets, the susceptibility of synthetic data to membership inference attacks, and the proposal of a practical and effective method to quantify membership disclosure risk using kernel density estimators. The document also touches on the background and related work of synthetic data generation, including various methodologies such as Bayesian networks and generative adversarial networks. The proposed method is compared to existing approaches, including computationally expensive shadow modeling strategies and distance-based methods, and is shown to achieve higher F1 scores and sharper risk characterization. The overall goal of the document is to provide a framework and metric for quantifying membership disclosure risk in synthetic data, enabling data custodians to conduct a post-generation risk assessment prior to releasing their synthetic datasets.

nodes.title: Membership Inference Attacks
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of quantifying membership disclosure risk in tabular synthetic data using kernel density estimators. The main points covered include the introduction of synthetic data generation as a privacy-preserving alternative to sharing real datasets, the susceptibility of synthetic data to membership inference attacks, and the proposal of a practical and effective method to quantify membership disclosure risk using kernel density estimators. The document also touches on the background and related work of synthetic data generation, including various methodologies such as Bayesian networks and generative adversarial networks. Additionally, it highlights the contributions of the proposed method, including a non-parametric, distance-based framework for probabilistic membership predictions, comprehensive validation across diverse datasets and generation models, and demonstration of superior F1 scores compared to existing methods.

nodes.title: Kernel Density Estimation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of quantifying membership disclosure risk in tabular synthetic data using kernel density estimators (KDEs). The main points covered include the introduction of synthetic data generation as a privacy-preserving alternative to sharing real datasets, the susceptibility of synthetic data to membership inference attacks (MIAs), and the proposal of a practical and effective method to quantify membership disclosure risk using KDEs. The document also covers background and related work on synthetic data generation, membership inference attacks, and kernel density estimation. The proposed method models the distribution of nearest-neighbour distances between synthetic data and training records, allowing probabilistic inference of membership and enabling robust evaluation via ROC curves. Two attack models are proposed: a 'True Distribution Attack' and a 'Realistic Attack', with empirical evaluations demonstrating the method's effectiveness in achieving higher F1 scores and sharper risk characterization than a prior baseline approach.

structure.title: Methodology
structure.summary: The partial document discusses synthetic data generation, membership inference attacks, and kernel density estimation. It highlights the limitations of existing methods for assessing privacy risks in synthetic data, including shadow modeling approaches and similarity-based methods. The document proposes a new approach based on kernel density estimation to model the relationship between nearest-neighbor distances and membership probability. The method is grounded on a distance-based approach and provides probabilistic membership predictions, offering a more comprehensive evaluation of membership inference risk. The document also introduces two variants of the proposed method: the True Distribution Attack and the Realistic Attack, which can be used to assess privacy risks in synthetic data.

nodes.title: Motivation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses synthetic data generation, membership inference attacks, and kernel density estimation. It highlights the limitations of existing methods for assessing privacy risks associated with synthetic data and machine learning models, particularly in large datasets. The document proposes a new approach based on kernel density estimation to quantify membership disclosure risk, which models the relationship between nearest-neighbour distances and membership probability. The method is grounded on a distance-based approach and provides probabilistic membership predictions, offering practical advantages in terms of computation time. The document also introduces two variants of the proposed method: the True Distribution Attack and the Realistic Attack, which can be used to assess membership inference risk in different scenarios.

nodes.title: true Distribution Attack
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the generation of synthetic data and the assessment of privacy risks associated with it. It highlights the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) and diffusion models for synthetic data generation. The document also focuses on membership inference attacks (MIAs) as a standard approach for evaluating privacy risks, including similarity-based approaches and shadow modeling approaches. Additionally, it introduces Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) as a nonparametric technique for estimating probability density functions. The document proposes a KDE-based approach for membership inference attacks, which models the relationship between nearest-neighbour distances and membership probability. Two variants of the approach are presented: the True Distribution Attack and the Realistic Attack. The True Distribution Attack uses real training data, synthetic data, and unseen holdout data to model membership probability, while the Realistic Attack uses auxiliary datasets accessible to adversaries. The document also discusses the evaluation of membership inference risk using metrics such as F1 score and ROC analysis. Overall, the partial document explores the use of KDE-based approaches for assessing privacy risks in synthetic data generation.

nodes.title: Realistic Attack
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a method for membership inference attacks on synthetic datasets. The main points covered include the computation of Gower's distance between each record in the attack dataset and its nearest neighbor in the synthetic dataset, and the use of separate kernel density estimates (KDEs) to model the distance distributions for member and non-member records. The document also introduces two attack variants: the true distribution attack, which assumes access to true membership labels, and the realistic attack, which assumes access to auxiliary datasets from the same population. The results of the attacks are evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, and ROC analysis on four publicly available datasets, including MIMIC-IV, UK Census, Texas-100X, and Nexoid COVID-19 data. The document also presents tables and figures showing the performance of the attacks across different datasets and generative models.

structure.title: Results
structure.summary: The partial document discusses membership inference attacks on synthetic datasets. It evaluates the performance of a realistic attack and a true distribution attack on four publicly available datasets using six different generative models. The results show that the realistic attack outperforms the true distribution attack in terms of F1 scores for certain thresholds and datasets. The document also presents the results of the attacks on each dataset, including accuracy and F1 scores, and compares the performance of different generative models. The main points covered include the process flow of the realistic attack, the evaluation methodology, and the results of the experiments on the four datasets.

It evaluates the performance of a realistic attack and a true distribution attack on four publicly available datasets using six different generative models. The results show that the realistic attack outperforms the true distribution attack in terms of F1 scores for certain thresholds and datasets. The document also presents the results of the attacks on each dataset, including accuracy and F1 scores, and compares the performance of different generative models. The main points covered include the process flow of the realistic attack, the evaluation methodology, and the results of the experiments on the four datasets.

nodes.title: true Distribution Attack
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses a method for membership inference attacks, specifically the True Distribution Attack and the Realistic Attack. The main points covered include: 
1. Computing Gower's distance between each record in the attack dataset and its nearest neighbor in the synthetic dataset to create a distance table.
2. Fitting separate Kernel Density Estimations (KDEs) to member distances and non-member distances to model the empirical distance distributions for each class.
3. Classifying test records by computing their nearest-neighbor distance to the synthetic dataset and estimating membership probability using the KDEs.
4. The use of Bayes' Theorem to formally justify the membership likelihood estimator.
5. The proposal of the Realistic Attack, where adversaries access only auxiliary datasets from the same population, without true membership labels, and simulate this using a mixture of training and non-training records.
6. The evaluation of the attacks using classification metrics, such as accuracy and F1 score, and comprehensive ROC analysis to assess the membership inference risk.

nodes.title: Realistic Attack
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a method for membership inference attacks on synthetic datasets. The main points covered include the computation of Gower's distance between each record in the attack dataset and its nearest neighbor in the synthetic dataset, and the use of separate kernel density estimates (KDEs) to model the distance distributions for members and non-members. The document also presents two attack variants: the true distribution attack, which assumes access to true membership labels, and the realistic attack, which assumes access to auxiliary datasets from the same population. The results of the attacks are evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, and ROC analysis on four publicly available datasets, including MIMIC-IV, UK Census, Texas-100X, and Nexoid COVID-19 data. The document also discusses the use of different generative models, such as CTGAN, ADS-GAN, and Bayesian Network, to generate synthetic datasets. Overall, the document presents a method for assessing membership inference attack risk on synthetic datasets and evaluating the effectiveness of different generative models in protecting against such attacks.

nodes.title: UK Census Data
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses membership inference attacks on synthetic datasets. It evaluates the performance of a realistic attack and a true distribution attack on four publicly available datasets using six different generative models. The results show that the realistic attack outperforms the true distribution attack in terms of F1 scores for certain thresholds and datasets. The document also presents the accuracy and F1 scores for the true distribution attack across various datasets and generative models, with the Bayesian Network generator observed to be more vulnerable to membership inference attacks. The results are visualized using heatmaps and plots to compare the performance of the realistic attack with another method. Overall, the document assesses the membership inference attack risk for synthetic datasets and evaluates the effectiveness of different attack methods.

nodes.title: Texas-100X and Nexoid Data
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a method for evaluating membership inference attacks on synthetic datasets. The main points covered include the use of Equation 1 to assess probabilistic membership, the impact of label noise on modeling inaccuracies, and the comparison of two attack variants: the true distribution attack and the realistic attack. The document also presents experimental results using four publicly available datasets and six different generative models, evaluating the attacks' performance using accuracy, F1 scores, and log-scaled ROC curves. The results show that the realistic attack can outperform the true distribution attack in certain cases, and that the choice of generative model and distance threshold can significantly affect the attack's performance. Additionally, the document highlights the importance of considering the realistic attack scenario, which takes into account the adversary's limited knowledge of the true membership labels.

structure.title: Discussion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the results of membership inference attacks (MIA) on various synthetic datasets, including MIMIC-IV and UK Census data. The main points covered include the comparison of true distribution attack and realistic attack methods, with results showing that TV AE-generated data is highly vulnerable to MIA. The document also presents figures illustrating the distribution of nearest-neighbour distances, distance versus membership probability relationships, and log-scaled ROC curves for true distribution attacks. Additionally, the results of realistic attacks are presented, showing limited separability between member and non-member records at lower thresholds. The document also touches on the MIA susceptibility of the UK Census dataset, which contains only categorical features, and shows reduced susceptibility to MIA attacks.

nodes.title: Distance Threshold vs. F1 Score for Realistic Attack
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the results of membership inference attacks (MIA) on various synthetic datasets, including MIMIC-IV and UK Census data. The main points covered include the comparison of true distribution attack and realistic attack methods, with results showing that TV AE-generated data is highly vulnerable to MIA. The document also presents figures illustrating the distribution of nearest-neighbour distances, distance versus membership probability relationships, and log-scaled ROC curves for true distribution attacks. Additionally, the results of realistic attacks are presented, showing limited separability between member and non-member records at lower thresholds. The document also touches on the MIA susceptibility of the UK Census dataset, which contains only categorical features, and shows reduced susceptibility to MIA attacks.

nodes.title: ROC Curve Analysis
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses membership inference attacks on synthetic datasets, specifically focusing on the evaluation of membership disclosure risk in tabular synthetic data. The document presents a computationally efficient framework for calculating membership disclosure risk using kernel density estimation (KDE) and nearest-neighbor distance distributions. The approach is applied to various synthetic datasets, including MIMIC-IV, UK Census, Texas-100X, and Nexoid data, and the results show that the proposed method can effectively assess membership inference risk. The document also explores the relationship between distance thresholds and F1 scores for realistic attacks, highlighting the importance of considering dataset properties and privacy characteristics of synthetic data generation methods. Additionally, the document discusses the limitations of average-case metrics, such as accuracy and AUC, in evaluating membership inference attacks and demonstrates the usefulness of ROC analysis in revealing critical vulnerabilities. Overall, the document proposes a novel framework for evaluating membership disclosure risk in synthetic datasets and highlights the need for careful consideration of dataset properties and privacy characteristics in assessing membership inference risk.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of membership disclosure risk in synthetic datasets, particularly in the context of generative models. It highlights the limitations of average metrics in assessing risk and presents a computationally efficient framework for calculating membership disclosure risk using kernel density estimation (KDE). The document also explores the use of log-ROC analysis to reveal substantial vulnerabilities in synthetic datasets, even when average metrics appear benign. Additionally, it touches on the importance of selecting appropriate metrics for evaluating membership disclosure risk, considering application-specific risk tolerance and privacy requirements. The conclusion summarizes the proposed KDE-based framework and suggests future work directions, including relaxing balanced dataset assumptions and developing hybrid strategies for more robust membership disclosure risk evaluation.
